skill.name: dicom-anonymizer
skill.description: De-identify DICOM medical images by removing PHI tags for research sharing, with audit logging and study-linkage preservation support.
skill.summary: Structured DICOM de-identification for medical research: removes PHI tags, generates audit logs, and preserves study linkage via deterministic UID hashing.
skill.author: AIPOCH

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported 77 potential issues at risk score 100/100, but AI evaluation confirms 74 are false positives. The 'Weak cryptographic algorithm' findings (24 locations) are false positives: SHA-256 is a strong, NIST-approved hash appropriate for one-way de-identification. The 'Ruby/shell backtick execution' findings (20 locations) are false positives: these are markdown code blocks showing example usage, not executable code. The 'Certificate/key files' findings are false positives referencing DICOM tag attributes (.keyword, .keys()). The 'System reconnaissance' findings are normal file I/O for a DICOM processing tool. Two patterns are true positives but demonstrably benign: audit log file writing (core feature) and a source URL in SKILL.md (attribution link). No credentials, no network connections, and no command injection vectors exist in executable code. The skill also explicitly refuses de-anonymization requests with a documented response template.

low_findings.title: Audit log file write operation
low_findings.description: scripts/main.py writes audit log JSON to user-specified output path. This is a core feature of the skill: generating compliance documentation for DICOM anonymization. The output path is specified by the user via --audit-log argument. No sensitive system files are written.

low_findings.title: Source repository URL in skill metadata
low_findings.description: SKILL.md contains a hardcoded URL pointing to the source GitHub repository. This is a standard source attribution link, not a network call. The executable code (scripts/main.py) makes no network connections.

dangerous_patterns.title: SHA-256 hash usage flagged as weak cryptography
dangerous_patterns.description: FALSE POSITIVE: Static scanner flagged hashlib.sha256() as a weak cryptographic algorithm. SHA-256 is a strong, NIST-approved cryptographic hash appropriate for one-way PHI de-identification. The skill uses it to generate deterministic hashes of patient IDs and DICOM UIDs. No reversible encryption is needed for this use case.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell commands in markdown documentation
dangerous_patterns.description: FALSE POSITIVE: Static scanner flagged shell commands in SKILL.md and audit-reference.md as Ruby/shell backtick execution. These are markdown code blocks demonstrating how to run the tool. The executable Python code (scripts/main.py) uses no subprocess, os.system, or shell execution functions.

dangerous_patterns.title: DICOM UID prefix flagged as hardcoded IP address
dangerous_patterns.description: FALSE POSITIVE: Static scanner flagged '1.2.826.0.1.3680043.999' as a hardcoded IP address. This is a DICOM UID prefix (Organization Root UID for research anonymization), not a network address. The dot-separated format resembles an IP address but serves a different purpose in DICOM protocol.

content.user_title: Anonymize DICOM Medical Images for Research
content.value_statement: Medical researchers need to share imaging data without exposing patient information. This skill removes PHI tags from DICOM files using SHA-256 hashing and provides audit logs for compliance documentation.
actual_capabilities: Remove PHI tags from single DICOM files or batch directories
Preserve study-series relationships using deterministic UID hashing
Generate JSON audit logs with anonymization details for compliance
Keep specific tags (such as PatientAge or StudyDate) while anonymizing others
Remove private manufacturer-specific tags to eliminate hidden identifiers
Refuse de-anonymization requests with documented institutional referral
limitations: Does not remove burned-in text or annotations from pixel data
Cannot certify HIPAA compliance or replace institutional privacy review
Requires pydicom Python library to execute the packaged script
Does not support de-anonymization: hashing is one-way by design

use_cases.title: Prepare imaging datasets for multi-site research collaboration
use_cases.description: A research group needs to share CT and MRI scans across institutions. Use the batch anonymization mode with study linkage preservation to remove all patient identifiers while maintaining study-series relationships for analysis.
use_cases.target_user: Medical researcher sharing imaging data across institutions

use_cases.title: Generate compliance documentation for data release
use_cases.description: A data manager must document which PHI tags were removed before releasing de-identified imaging data. Generate an audit log with per-file anonymization records, tag actions, and pseudonym mappings for institutional compliance review.

use_cases.title: Anonymize single DICOM files for publication supplements
use_cases.description: A journal requires de-identified images for a publication supplement. Anonymize individual DICOM files while keeping demographic fields like PatientAge and PatientSex for study context.
use_cases.target_user: Clinical researcher submitting de-identified images to journals

prompt_templates.title: Anonymize a single DICOM file
prompt_templates.prompt: Anonymize the DICOM file at [input_path] and save to [output_path]. Generate an audit log.
prompt_templates.scenario: User provides a single DICOM file path and output destination

prompt_templates.title: Batch anonymize a directory with linkage preservation
prompt_templates.prompt: Batch anonymize all DICOM files in [input_directory] and save to [output_directory]. Preserve study linkages and generate an audit log.
prompt_templates.scenario: User has a folder of DICOM files from multiple studies

prompt_templates.title: Custom tag preservation during anonymization
prompt_templates.prompt: Anonymize the DICOM file at [input_path] but keep these tags: [list of tags such as PatientAge, StudyDate]. Save output to [output_path].
prompt_templates.scenario: User needs to retain specific DICOM tags for research purposes

prompt_templates.title: Generate anonymization plan without execution
prompt_templates.prompt: Review the DICOM file at [input_path] and produce an anonymization plan. List which PHI tags will be cleared, which will be hashed, and which will be preserved. Do not execute the anonymization.
prompt_templates.scenario: User wants to preview what will happen before running the tool

output_examples.input: Anonymize scan.dcm to anonymized.dcm with audit log.
Batch anonymize ./dicoms/ to ./anon/ with study linkage.
output: Study linkage preserved via deterministic UID hashing

best_practices: Always generate an audit log when anonymizing data for research use. The audit log provides a record of which tags were modified and supports compliance reviews.
Verify that pydicom is installed before running the script. Run 'python -m py_compile scripts/main.py' to check for syntax errors before starting a batch anonymization.
Run a manual quality check on anonymized output to verify no burned-in annotations remain in pixel data and that preserved tags do not contain residual PHI.
anti_patterns: Do not claim HIPAA compliance certification after anonymization. This tool removes DICOM metadata tags but does not verify pixel data or approve institutional release.
Do not attempt de-anonymization or reversal of hashed patient IDs. SHA-256 hashing is one-way by design and the original values are not stored by this tool.
Do not skip the manual QA step before sharing anonymized data externally. Private manufacturer tags, burned-in annotations, and sequence-specific identifiers may still contain PHI.

faq.question: What DICOM tags does this tool anonymize?
faq.answer: The tool anonymizes over 50 standard PHI tags including PatientName, PatientID, PatientBirthDate, institution names, physician names, and device serial numbers. PatientSex, PatientAge, and imaging parameters are preserved for research use.

faq.question: Can I reverse the anonymization to recover patient data?
faq.answer: No. Anonymization is irreversible by design. Patient IDs and UIDs are hashed using one-way SHA-256. The original values are not stored by the tool and cannot be recovered from the anonymized output.

faq.question: Does this tool guarantee HIPAA compliance?
faq.answer: No. This tool assists with DICOM metadata de-identification but does not provide legal certification. Institutional privacy review and manual QA of pixel data are still required before external release.

faq.question: What happens if pydicom is not installed?
faq.answer: The script detects missing pydicom and provides a manual anonymization checklist as a fallback. It does not claim successful anonymization when the dependency is absent.

faq.question: Can I keep some tags while anonymizing others?
faq.answer: Yes. Use the --keep-tags parameter with tag names such as PatientAge or StudyDate. The tool will preserve those tags while anonymizing all other PHI tags.

faq.question: Does this remove text burned into the image pixels?
faq.answer: No. The tool only modifies DICOM metadata tags. Text or annotations burned into the pixel data cannot be removed. Manual inspection of pixel data is required before sharing.
